analogy: Think of "Print Culture and the Modern World" through the chapter's own opening images: SECTION III EVERYDAY LIFE, CULTURE AND POLITICS 105 Print Culture It is difficult for us to imagine a world without printed matter. We find evidence of print everywhere around us – in books, journals, newspapers, prints of famous paintings, and also in everyday things like... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Print Culture and the Modern World is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that sECTION III EVERYDAY LIFE, CULTURE AND POLITICS

The text explains that 105 Print Culture It is difficult for us to imagine a world without printed matter.

The text explains that we find evidence of print everywhere around us – in books, journals, newspapers, prints of famous paintings, and also in everyday things like theatre programmes, official circulars, calendars, diaries, advertisements, cinema posters at street corners.

The text explains that we read printed literature, see printed images, follow the news through newspapers, and track public debates that appear in print.

The text explains that we take for granted this world of print and often forget that there was a time before print.

The text explains that we may not realise that print itself has a history which has, in fact, shaped our contemporary world.

The text explains that when did printed literature begin to circulate?

The text explains that in this chapter we will look at the development of print, from its beginnings in East Asia to its expansion in Europe and in India.

The text explains that we will understand the impact of the spread of technology and consider how social lives and cultures changed with the coming of print.

The text explains that p r i n t C u l t u r e a n d t h e M o d e r n W o r l d Chapter V Print Culture and the Modern World Fig.

The text explains that 1 – Book making before the age of print, from Akhlaq-i-Nasiri, 1595.

The text explains that this is a royal workshop in the sixteenth century, much before printing began in India.

The text explains that you can see the text being dictated, written and illustrated.

The text explains that the art of writing and illustrating by hand was important in the age before print.

The text explains that think about what happened to these forms of art with the coming of printing machines.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 106 1 The First Printed Books Fig.

The text explains that it may be that one here and there contributes something worth knowing, but the very multitude of them is hurtful to scholarship, because it creates a glut, and even in good things satiety is most harmful ... [printers] fill the world with books, not just trifling things (such as I write, perhaps), but stupid, ignorant, slanderous, scandalous, raving, irreligious and seditious books, and the number of them is such that even the valuable publications lose their value.’ Source Source A Fig.

The text explains that the interior of the printer’s workshop here is the site of a dance of death.

The text explains that source Source B New words Despotism – A system of governance in which absolute power is exercised by an individual, unregulated by legal and constitutional checks

The text explains that they were not influenced directly by everything they read or saw.

The text explains that print did not directly shape their minds, but it did open up the possibility of thinking differently.

The text explains that you can almost hear the British master say – ‘This is the model, produce Indian versions of it.’ Source Source E In 1926, Begum Rokeya Sakhawat Hossein, a noted educationist and literary figure, strongly condemned men for withholding education from women in the name of religion as she addressed the Bengal Women’s Education Conference: ‘The opponents of female education say that women will become unruly … Fie!

The text explains that strangely, its early measures to control printed matter were directed against Englishmen in India who were critical of Company misrule and hated the actions of particular Company officers.

The text explains that source Source F Gandhi said in 1922: ‘Liberty of speech ... liberty of the press ... freedom of association.

The text explains that why did some people in eighteenth century Europe think that print culture would bring enlightenment and end despotism?

The text explains that why did some people fear the effect of easily available printed books?

The text explains that choose one example from Europe and one from India.

The text explains that what were the effects of the spread of print culture for poor people in nineteenth century India?

The text explains that explain how print culture assisted the growth of nationalism in India.

The text explains that find out more about the changes in print technology in the last 100 years.

The text explains that write about the changes, explaining why they have taken place, what their consequences have been.

The text explains that give reasons for the following: a) Woodblock print only came to Europe after 1295. b) Martin Luther was in favour of print and spoke out in praise of it. c) The Roman Catholic Church began keeping an Index of Prohibited books from the mid-sixteenth century. d) Gandhi said the fight for Swaraj is a fight for liberty of speech, liberty of the press, and freedom of association.

The text explains that write short notes to show what you know about: a) The Gutenberg Press b) Erasmus’s idea of the printed book c) The Vernacular Press Act 3.

The text explains that what did the spread of print culture in nineteenth century India mean to: a) Women b) The poor c) Reformers Write in brief Project

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 5: Print Culture and the Modern World

prelims_traps: Do not treat SECTION III and EVERYDAY LIFE, CULTURE AND POLITICS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Print Culture and the Modern World; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Print Culture and the Modern World through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use SECTION III and EVERYDAY LIFE, CULTURE AND POLITICS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

India and the Contemporary World 106 1 The First Printed Books Fig.
The earliest kind of print technology was developed in China, Japan and Korea.
From AD 594 onwards, books in China were printed by rubbing paper – also invented there – against the inked surface of woodblocks.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Print Culture and the Modern World, which statement is supported by the source text about 2 Print Comes to Europe?
options: As both sides of the thin, porous sheet could not be printed, the traditional Chinese ‘accordion book’ was folded and stitched at the side.
Superbly skilled craftsmen could duplicate, with remarkable accuracy, the beauty of calligraphy.
The imperial state in China was, for a very long time, the major producer of printed material.
China possessed a huge bureaucratic system which recruited its personnel through civil service examinations.
